arguments.command: which addr2line llvm-symbolizer llvm-addr2line 2>/dev/null; apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'binutils|llvm' | head -5

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
# Try downloading the debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server
# BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"
WORKDIR="/tmp/sv-21d1d8e473364061b5a76d72a26e5862/symbols"
mkdir -p "$WORKDIR"

# Try the ELF debug info symbol server URL format
echo "=== Trying debug symbols ==="
curl -sSL -o "$WORKDIR/_.debug" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" 2>&1

echo "=== Trying library binary ==="
curl -sSL -o "$WORKDIR/libmonosgen-2.0.so" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" 2>&1

ls -la "$WORKDIR/"
arguments.description: Download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in this tombstone. The key frames are from `libmonosgen-2.0.so` with BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be`.

To symbolicate these frames, I would need:
1. The debug symbols for this specific build of `libmonosgen-2.0.so` (matching the BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be`)
2. A tool like `addr2line`, `llvm-symbolizer`, or similar to resolve the addresses to function names and source locations


3. I can look up the symbols on Microsoft's symbol server using the BuildId to construct the proper URL and download the matching debug symbols, then use a symbolication tool to resolve the PC addresses to function names and source locations. Let me check what debugging tools are available and try downloading the symbols using the correct URL patterns for ELF binaries and their debug info.

arguments.command: which addr2line llvm-symbolizer llvm-addr2line 2>/dev/null; apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'binutils|llvm' | head -5

BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"
WORKDIR="/tmp/sv-21d1d8e473364061b5a76d72a26e5862/symbols"
mkdir -p "$WORKDIR"

# Try the ELF debug info symbol server URL format
echo "=== Trying debug symbols ==="
curl -sSL -o "$WORKDIR/_.debug" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" 2>&1

echo "=== Trying library binary ==="
curl -sSL -o "$WORKDIR/libmonosgen-2.0.so" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" 2>&1

ls -la "$WORKDIR/"

data.reasoningText:
Both the debug symbols and the library binary were downloaded successfully. Now I need to use `addr2line` to symbolicate the frames. But `addr2line` on this system is for x86_64, while the library is ARM64 (aarch64). Let me check the file types and see if we need a cross-architecture toolchain.

Actually, `addr2line` works on debug info (DWARF) which is architecture-independent for the purpose of address-to-line mapping. Let me verify the files and then try to symbolicate.

Wait, actually addr2line needs to understand the ELF format of the target architecture. The x86_64 binutils might not handle aarch64 ELF files. Let me check.
 
Modern binutils from Ubuntu often support multiple architectures, so let me just try it. I have the PC addresses from the crash log that I need to map back to source locations.
